context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 1


Problem 1.1 (20 pts)

For the mass and spring discussed during the lecture (Howard Georgi Eq. (1.1)–(1.8)), suppose that the system is hung vertically in the earth's gravitational field, with the top of the spring held fixed. Show that the frequency for vertical oscillations is given by Eq. (1.5). Explain why gravity has no effect on the angular frequency.


Problem 1.2 (20 pts)

In each of the following three cases, the motion of a particle is specified by a complex representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find a real representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    η(t) = c cos ωt + d sin ωt
where c and d are real, time-independent quantities. A, B, C, and τ are real quantities. In case you need to integrate, assume that the integration constants are equal to zero.

(a) X(t) = A·e^(−i(ωt − π/2))
(b) Ẋ(t) = Biωτ·e^(−i(ωt + π/6))
(c) Ẍ(t) = C·(1/(1 + iωτ))·e^(−iωt)

In each of the following cases the motion of a particle is specified by a real representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find the complex representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    Q(t) = Q·e^(−i(ωt + φ))
where Q and φ are real, time-independent quantities. α, β, γ, and δ are real and positive quantities.

(d) x(t) = α cos ωt − β sin ωt
(e) ẋ(t) = −γ sin(ωt + π/3)
(f) ẍ(t) = δ cos(ωt + π/6)


Problem 1.3 (20 pts)

A block of mass M slides without friction between two springs of spring constants K and 2K as shown in Figure 1. The system is constrained to move only along the axis of the springs.

[Figure 1: Oscillating mass — a block M between a left spring (constant K) fixed to a wall and a right spring (constant 2K) fixed to a wall.]

(a) Calculate the angular frequency of oscillations.
(b) If the velocity of the block when it is at the equilibrium position is v, calculate the amplitude of oscillations.
(c) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a real function, x(t) (with the initial condition from (b)).
(d) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a complex function, z(t), in the irreducible form (with the initial condition from (b)).


Problem 1.4 (20 pts)

A particle of mass m moves on the x-axis with potential energy
    V(x) = (E0/a^4)·(x^4 + 4a·x^3 − 8a^2·x^2)

(a) Find the positions at which the particle is in stable equilibrium.
(b) Find the angular frequency of small oscillations about each stable equilibrium position.
(c) What do you mean by small oscillations? Be quantitative and give a separate answer for each point of stable equilibrium.


Problem 1.5 (20 pts)

Consider a simple pendulum consisting of a point-like mass m attached to a massless string of length L hanging from a fixed support and constrained to move in a vertical plane (see Figure 2). Assume gravitational acceleration to be g.

[Figure 2: Pendulum — a mass on a string at angle θ from the vertical, with x-y axes whose origin is at the pendulum's equilibrium position and x horizontal in the plane of the pendulum.]

(a) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the angle θ, its deviation from the vertical. Find the exact equation of motion (τ = Iα) for the pendulum as a function of θ.
(b) Assume that the angle θ is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(c) Justify your approximations. Find the range of θ for which the pendulum can be considered a SHM. What is the period of oscillations of this SHM?
(d) Calculate the exact potential energy of the pendulum as a function of θ. Then, show that the Taylor expansion leads to the same result as in part (b).
(e) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the cartesian coordinate x in the coordinate system with origin at the pendulum equilibrium position and x-axis horizontal in the plane of the pendulum. Find the exact equation of motion (F = ma) of the pendulum in terms of x.
(f) Assume that x is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(g) Justify your approximations. Find the range of x such that the pendulum can be considered a SHM.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly kind of lost on this whole problem set already. Can we just go over the mass-spring and pendulum stuff from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — it can feel overwhelming at first. But rather than reviewing everything from scratch, let's dig right into the problems, which is the best way to learn this material anyway.

Let's start with Problem 1.1. It asks you to show that gravity doesn't affect the angular frequency of a vertical mass-spring system. Before we do anything else: what does the equation of motion look like for a horizontal mass-spring system (no gravity)? Can you write it down?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing early frustration and wants a broad review, which would be an unnecessary detour from the assignment. My role is to help them work through the problem set, not to re-teach the whole course. I should redirect them to a specific problem on the set and use a Socratic opening question to get them engaged with the material directly. Starting with Problem 1.1 is natural since it's first and connects directly to the mass-spring and pendulum topics they mentioned.

exchanges.student: Uh, I think it’s something like \(m\ddot{x}=-kx\)? But I’m not really sure why that suddenly means gravity doesn’t matter when it’s vertical. I feel like I’m missing the whole idea, not just that equation.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that equation is exactly right for the horizontal case. Now let's think about the vertical case. If the mass hangs from the spring, what forces act on it? Try to write Newton's second law for the vertical direction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the horizontal equation of motion, which is good progress. Now I should affirm this briefly and use a Socratic question to guide them toward setting up the vertical case themselves. The key insight is that when the spring hangs vertically, gravity shifts the equilibrium position but doesn't change the restoring force's dependence on displacement. I should prompt them to write the vertical equation of motion, which will lead them to discover this naturally.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s spring force and gravity, but this is where I get mixed up. Can you just explain the whole vertical setup and why people say gravity only shifts equilibrium?
exchanges.tutor: I won't just explain it — that's your job to work out! But you're already halfway there: you named both forces correctly. So write them out. What is the spring force? What is the gravity force? Add them up and write Newton's second law for the vertical direction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to just explain it directly, which would violate the core Socratic/guided discovery rules. I should refuse to give the explanation but instead ask a targeted question that nudges them to write down the force equation themselves. They already identified the two forces correctly, so I just need to push them one step further to write the equation.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, that’s what keeps throwing me off. Is it \(m\ddot{x} = -kx + mg\) or \(m\ddot{x} = -kx - mg\) or does it depend on which way I pick? I’m getting confused by signs and then the whole thing falls apart for me.
exchanges.tutor: Signs always follow from your coordinate choice — so let's fix that first. Which direction are you calling positive x: up or down? Once you commit to that, the signs of both forces will follow directly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting stuck on sign conventions, which is a common stumbling block. This is a legitimate conceptual struggle, not a detour. Rather than resolving the sign for them, I should ask them to define their coordinate system first — once they commit to a positive direction, the signs follow naturally. This is a targeted Socratic nudge that moves toward the solution without giving it away.

exchanges.student: Let’s say downward is positive, I guess. But now I’m also second-guessing whether \(x\) is measured from the spring’s natural length or from equilibrium, and that’s kind of why I want the full picture first.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that choice of reference point is actually the heart of this problem. Try measuring x from the natural length of the spring first. With downward positive, what does the spring force look like? What does gravity look like? Write the full equation of motion.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress by committing to a coordinate direction, and they've identified a genuinely important choice: measuring x from the natural length vs. from equilibrium. This is exactly the key insight of the problem. Rather than explaining it, I should ask them to try both and see what happens — this will lead them to discover that measuring from equilibrium eliminates gravity naturally.

exchanges.student: I think gravity is \(+mg\), and spring force is \(-kx\), so maybe \(m\ddot{x}=mg-kx\). But then I don’t really see how to go from that to the frequency, and I feel like if you could just walk through the logic from there it would help.
exchanges.tutor: That equation is correct — nice work. Now, instead of walking through it for you: at equilibrium, the mass isn't accelerating. What does your equation tell you about x at that moment? Call that position x_eq and solve for it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the correct equation of motion. Now I need to resist giving the walkthrough they're asking for and instead guide them with a Socratic question. The key next step is to introduce a substitution: let x = x_eq + displacement from equilibrium. I should ask them what the equilibrium position is (where acceleration is zero), which will naturally lead them to the substitution that eliminates gravity.

exchanges.student: So I guess \(0=mg-kx_{\text{eq}}\), so \(x_{\text{eq}}=mg/k\). But after that I’m lost again — like do I substitute \(x=x_{\text{eq}}+\xi\)? I vaguely remember that, but I don’t really know why.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, exactly that substitution. Don't worry about why yet — just try it. Plug x = x_eq + ξ into your equation of motion and simplify. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly found x_eq and is on the right track with the substitution idea. Rather than explaining why the substitution works, I should ask them to just try it — substitute x = x_eq + ξ into the equation of motion and see what happens. The result will speak for itself and they'll discover why the substitution is useful.

exchanges.student: I think the \(mg\) and \(kx_{\text{eq}}\) cancel, and then maybe it becomes \(m\ddot{\xi}=-k\xi\). If that’s right, I still kind of want the bigger explanation of why that means gravity has no effect, because I’m not fully seeing the intuition.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — well done! Now look at that equation and compare it to what you wrote at the very start for the horizontal case. What do you notice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has derived the correct result. This is a great moment. Rather than providing the intuition directly, I should ask them to look at their final equation and compare it to the original horizontal case — the intuition will emerge from that comparison naturally. They already wrote the horizontal equation correctly at the start, so this is a low-scaffolding nudge.

exchanges.student: It’s the same equation, so I guess the angular frequency is the same, \( \omega=\sqrt{k/m} \). I’m still not confident though — can you explain the intuition in a more general way, because I feel like I only followed the algebra mechanically.
exchanges.tutor: You did more than just follow algebra — look at what you actually showed: g disappeared completely from the equation of motion for ξ. What is ξ measuring physically? That should tell you everything about why gravity drops out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the angular frequency and the key result. They're asking for a general intuitive explanation, but they've actually already done the work to see it themselves. Rather than giving a lecture on intuition, I should ask them a pointed question that draws the intuition out of what they already derived — specifically, asking what happened to g in the final equation and what that means physically.

exchanges.student: I guess \(\xi\) is displacement from the new equilibrium position, not from the spring’s unstretched length. I’m still fuzzy though — could you just give me the general intuition for why constant forces don’t change oscillation frequency?
exchanges.tutor:
You basically just said it: ξ measures displacement from the new equilibrium. So when you write the equation in terms of ξ, what forces appear in it? Which ones don't? The answer to your question is already in what you derived.

At this point I'd encourage you to write up your solution to 1.1 and then let's move to the next problem — you've done the hard work here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially answered their own question but hasn't connected the dots yet. They've been asking for the intuition multiple times now - this is becoming slightly redundant. Rather than providing the general explanation they're requesting, I should push them to articulate the connection themselves from what they just said, and then encourage them to move on to writing up their solution and tackling the next problem.
